findings.description: Full card number (PAN) and CVV are written to the application log in plaintext, a serious PCI-DSS violation and credential-leak vector.
CVV is handled/logged at all; PCI-DSS prohibits storing or persisting CVV even transiently in logs.
monthly_revenue_report recomputes revenue as raw unit_price*quantity without the per-line discount or tax used at charge time, so reported revenue will not match amounts actually charged.
Transaction IDs are derived from Python's non-cryptographic hash() of (last4, amount), making them weak, low-entropy, and collision-prone rather than from a secure unique source.

No idempotency key or post-charge failure handling: if gateway.charge succeeds but the subsequent UPDATE/commit fails, the customer is charged while the order stays unpaid, and a retry double-charges.
compute_order_total returns an unrounded float (tax multiplication not rounded to cents), so the returned dollar total carries floating-point artifacts.
Monetary math uses binary float then int(round(total*100)); float representation and banker's rounding can produce off-by-one-cent amounts—money should use Decimal.
discount_pct is not bounds-checked, so a value >100 yields a negative line price and a value <0 inflates the total.
N+1 query pattern: a separate order_items query is issued per order in the report instead of a single JOIN/GROUP BY aggregate.
charge does not validate card_number/cvv format or non-emptiness; an empty card_number makes card_number[-4:] an empty slice on line 38.
API key is retained in plaintext as an instance attribute with no protection or redaction guard.
charge_order uses SELECT * rather than enumerating the needed columns, coupling the code to table layout.
A new SQLite connection is opened per public call with no pooling/reuse, adding per-operation connection overhead.
On a failed charge the function returns the result without closing/aborting any pending transaction state explicitly; relies solely on finally-close with no rollback.
